event: Paleolithic hunter-gatherers leave rock engravings in Badanj Cave near Stolac, among the oldest evidence of human habitation in the region.
Neolithic farming communities of the Butmir culture flourish near present-day Sarajevo, producing distinctive spiral-decorated pottery.
Illyrian tribes, including the Daesitiates and Autariatae, settle the region during the Bronze and Iron Ages, building hillforts across the highlands.
Rome completes the brutal suppression of the Great Illyrian Revolt and incorporates the territory into the province of Dalmatia.
Roman rule brings roads, mining of silver and lead, and towns such as Domavia to the region of present-day Bosnia.
The partition of the Roman Empire places the region near the dividing line between its Western and Eastern halves, shaping its later cultural frontier.
Slavic peoples migrate into the Balkans, settling Bosnia and gradually assimilating or displacing the earlier Romanized Illyrian population.
Byzantine emperor Constantine VII Porphyrogennetos mentions 'Bosona' in De Administrando Imperio, the earliest documented reference to Bosnia as a land.
Ban Kulin begins his rule, ushering in a prosperous and largely independent Banate of Bosnia.
Ban Kulin issues a charter granting trade privileges to merchants of Ragusa, one of the oldest documents of Bosnian statehood.
Ban Kulin and Bosnian church leaders formally renounce heresy at Bilino Polje amid papal pressure over the dualist Bosnian Church.
Tvrtko I is crowned King of Bosnia, elevating the banate to a kingdom that becomes a leading power in the western Balkans.
King Tvrtko I dies after expanding Bosnian territory toward the Adriatic, leaving the kingdom to fracture among rival nobles.
Magnate Stjepan Vukcic Kosaca adopts the title 'Herzog of Saint Sava,' giving the region of Herzegovina its enduring name.
Ottoman forces conquer the Kingdom of Bosnia and execute the last king, Stjepan Tomasevic, beginning centuries of Ottoman rule.
The Ottomans complete their conquest of Herzegovina, fully absorbing the region into the empire.
Sarajevo flourishes as an Ottoman administrative and commercial center, with mosques, bazaars, and public works transforming the urban landscape.
Construction is completed on the Old Bridge (Stari Most) at Mostar, a masterpiece of Ottoman engineering by architect Mimar Hayruddin.
Habsburg general Eugene of Savoy raids and burns Sarajevo during the Great Turkish War, devastating the city.
Husein Gradascevic leads a major revolt of Bosnian nobles against Ottoman centralizing reforms, demanding autonomy before being defeated.
The Herzegovinian peasant uprising against Ottoman taxation ignites a wider Balkan crisis among the great powers.
The Congress of Berlin places Bosnia and Herzegovina under Austro-Hungarian occupation and administration while it nominally remains Ottoman.
Austria-Hungary formally annexes Bosnia and Herzegovina, triggering an international crisis with Serbia and Russia.
Gavrilo Princip assassinates Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo, the spark that ignites World War I.
Following Austria-Hungary's collapse, Bosnia and Herzegovina joins the new Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes, later Yugoslavia.
Bosnia is incorporated into the fascist Independent State of Croatia, and brutal occupation, mass killings, and partisan resistance follow during World War II.
Tito's Partisans hold the second AVNOJ session in Jajce, laying the foundations for a federal socialist Yugoslavia that includes Bosnia as a republic.
Bosnia and Herzegovina becomes one of the six constituent republics of socialist Yugoslavia after the Partisan victory.
Ivo Andric, the Yugoslav writer associated with Bosnia, wins the Nobel Prize in Literature for works including 'The Bridge on the Drina.'
Sarajevo hosts the Winter Olympic Games, a showcase moment of Yugoslav-era pride and international prestige.
Bosnia and Herzegovina declares independence after a referendum, is admitted to the United Nations, and is engulfed by war.
The Siege of Sarajevo begins, becoming the longest siege of a capital city in modern warfare and lasting nearly four years.
Mostar's Old Bridge is destroyed by shelling during the Croat-Bosniak conflict, a symbol of the war's cultural devastation.
Bosnian Serb forces murder over 8,000 Bosniak men and boys at Srebrenica in a genocide, the worst atrocity in Europe since World War II.
The Dayton Peace Agreement ends the Bosnian War, creating a state of two entities: the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina and Republika Srpska.
The reconstructed Old Bridge of Mostar reopens, symbolizing postwar reconciliation, and is later inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Bosnia and Herzegovina joins NATO's Partnership for Peace program, deepening its Euro-Atlantic integration.
Bosnia and Herzegovina signs a Stabilisation and Association Agreement with the European Union, a key step toward membership.
Widespread anti-government protests and citizen assemblies ('plenums') erupt over economic stagnation, corruption, and unemployment.
Bosnia and Herzegovina submits its formal application for European Union membership.
The European Union grants Bosnia and Herzegovina official candidate status for EU membership.
The European Council agrees to open EU accession negotiations with Bosnia and Herzegovina, marking a milestone in its integration path.
